notes: Every finding evaluated as if this were a real production deployment (training intent disregarded). Verified by reading source files at the cited line numbers.

findings.title: SQL Injection / Authentication Bypass in login endpoint
findings.code_snippet: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '${security.hash(req.body.password || '')}' AND deletedAt IS NULL`, { model: UserModel, plain: true })
findings.exploitation_rationale: POST /rest/user/login passes req.body.email directly into a raw template literal SQL query. Sending email='\' OR 1=1--' is a textbook tautology bypass that returns the first row (the admin) and the route then issues a JWT for that user via security.authorize(). No parameterization, no escaping, no allow-list. The result is full admin takeover from an entirely unauthenticated request.

proof_of_concept.expected_response: JSON containing { authentication: { token: <JWT for admin@juice-sh.op>, bid: ... } }

findings.recommended_fix: Use parameterized queries via Sequelize replacements/bind (e.g. UserModel.findOne({ where: { email, password: hashedPw } })) and migrate password handling to bcrypt/argon2. Never interpolate untrusted strings into raw SQL.

findings.title: UNION SQL Injection in unauthenticated product search
findings.code_snippet: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`)
findings.exploitation_rationale: GET /rest/products/search?q= is reachable without authentication and concatenates the query parameter directly into raw SQL. Because the database is SQLite the attacker can append a UNION SELECT statement to exfiltrate Users (emails, MD5 password hashes) and sqlite_master (table schemas). The route code itself solves a 'unionSqlInjectionChallenge' by detecting if user rows leak via this primitive, which proves it is exploitable end-to-end.

proof_of_concept.request: GET /rest/products/search?q=')) UNION SELECT id,email,password,role,1,2,3,4,5 FROM Users--
proof_of_concept.expected_response: Product list response includes every user's email and md5 password hash.

findings.recommended_fix: Use Sequelize replacements/bind (`:criteria`) or ProductModel.findAll({ where: { [Op.or]: [{ name: { [Op.like]: q } }, ...] } }). The 200-byte truncation does not prevent injection.

findings.title: Hard-coded RSA private key used to sign all JWTs
findings.code_snippet: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\nMIICXAIBAAKBgQDNwqLEe9wgTXCbC7+RPdDbBbeqjdbs4kOPOIGzqLpXvJXlxxW8...\r\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
findings.exploitation_rationale: The full 1024-bit RSA private key used by security.authorize() to sign all session JWTs (RS256) is checked into source. Anyone who reads the public GitHub repo can mint a JWT with `data.role="admin"` for any user id and bypass every authentication and authorization check in the application. The matching `encryptionkeys/jwt.pub` is the corresponding public key used for verification. This is the single most catastrophic finding.

proof_of_concept.snippet: jwt.sign({ data: { id: 1, email: 'admin@juice-sh.op', role: 'admin' } }, privateKey, { algorithm: 'RS256', expiresIn: '6h' })

findings.recommended_fix: Generate a fresh RSA-2048 (or Ed25519) key pair at deploy time, store the private key in a secret manager (KMS/Vault/SSM), rotate immediately, and revoke any tokens minted under the leaked key.

findings.title: MD5 used as password hash with no salt
findings.reachability: post-disclosure (e.g. after UNION SQLi in JS-AUDIT-002)
findings.exploitation_rationale: User passwords are stored as bare MD5 hashes (no salt, no key-stretching). MD5 is broken for password hashing — modern GPUs hash hundreds of GH/s. Combined with the UNION SQLi (JS-AUDIT-002), every password becomes trivially crackable by rainbow tables / hashcat. Even without SQLi, the hashing function is used in 'authorize'-equivalent flows server-side.
findings.recommended_fix: Switch to bcrypt (cost >= 12), argon2id, or scrypt with per-user salt. Rehash on next login.

findings.title: Hard-coded HMAC secret used for security-answer verification
findings.exploitation_rationale: Security-answer 'hashes' in SecurityAnswers table are HMAC-SHA256 with a static, public secret. With the secret in the repo any attacker who reads the SecurityAnswers row can precompute HMACs offline (or just submit known answers) to reset a victim's password via /rest/user/reset-password (routes/resetPassword.ts:41 compares `security.hmac(answer) === data.answer`). It also disables HMAC's collision resistance assumption.
findings.recommended_fix: Move secret to env/secret manager; rotate. Long-term, do not rely on security questions; require a verified-email token flow instead.

findings.title: express-jwt 0.1.3 + jsonwebtoken 0.4.0 — alg:none / verification bypass
findings.code_snippet: export const isAuthorized = () => expressJwt(({ secret: publicKey }) as any)
findings.exploitation_rationale: package.json pins express-jwt@0.1.3 and jsonwebtoken@0.4.0 — both are wildly out of date. express-jwt prior to 5.3.3 (GHSA-6cwn-77pq-3fpx) does not enforce `algorithms`, so an attacker can craft a token with header `alg:none` (no signature) and bypass auth on every middleware-protected route. jsonwebtoken<=4.2.1 has the original verification-bypass CVE. Combined with the hardcoded public key being downloadable from /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub, the attacker can also forge HS256 tokens signed with the public key as a symmetric secret.
findings.recommended_fix: Upgrade express-jwt to >=8.x (passing `algorithms: ['RS256']`) and jsonwebtoken to >=9.x. Pin algorithm explicitly.

findings.title: denyAll() relies on Math.random() as JWT verification secret
findings.code_snippet: export const denyAll = () => expressJwt({ secret: '' + Math.random() } as any)
findings.exploitation_rationale: `denyAll` is wired into critical routes (PUT/DELETE /api/Users/:id, DELETE /api/Products/:id, /api/Challenges, /api/Hints, /api/SecurityQuestions, /api/Recycles/:id, etc.). Because of the express-jwt 0.1.3 alg:none bug above, a token with `alg:none` would skip secret validation entirely. Even without that, Math.random() is non-cryptographic and predictable; an attacker who can guess one secret can mint tokens. The bigger issue is that 'deny all' is implemented as 'verify with a random secret' instead of a hard 403, which is fundamentally wrong.
findings.recommended_fix: Replace with an explicit middleware that always returns 403: `(req,res)=>res.status(403).end()`. Do not rely on the JWT layer at all.

findings.title: Open-redirect allowlist uses substring match (url.includes)
findings.exploitation_rationale: isRedirectAllowed() returns true if the *attacker-supplied URL* contains an allowlisted string anywhere. Therefore `/redirect?to=https://evil.example.com/?x=https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop` passes the check and the server returns a 302 to evil.example.com. Used for phishing / credential harvesting / token exfil. CodeQL (alert 3) confirms.
findings.recommended_fix: Parse with `new URL(toUrl)` and compare `url.origin` against an allowlist of full origins; or use signed redirect tokens.

findings.title: Pug SSTI + eval() on username + user-controlled CSP -> RCE/XSS
findings.code_snippet: username = eval(code) // eslint-disable-line no-eval  (code is parsed out of #{...} in user.username)
findings.reachability: external (any authenticated user can set their own username)
findings.exploitation_rationale: The route reads the logged-in user's own username from the DB. If it matches `#{...}` the inner expression is passed to JavaScript `eval()` *server-side*. Any authenticated user can therefore execute arbitrary Node.js code in the server process by updating their own username via PATCH /api/Users/:id or POST /profile. Additionally, the `profileImage` field is interpolated unsanitised into a Content-Security-Policy header (line 88), letting the same user weaken/override CSP and achieve persistent XSS. This is full server-side RCE.

proof_of_concept.step2_trigger: GET /profile  with valid cookie -> response renders attacker output

findings.recommended_fix: Never eval user data. Render usernames with HTML-escape only (Handlebars/Angular auto-escape). Build CSP from a static template, never interpolate user input.

findings.title: B2B order safeEval (notevil) used inside vm with attacker-controlled body -> RCE/DoS
findings.reachability: authenticated (B2B requires JWT — but JWT can be forged per JS-AUDIT-003)
findings.exploitation_rationale: `notevil` (1.3.3) has had multiple documented sandbox escapes (e.g. accessing constructor chains via prototype lookups to reach `Function('return process')()`). It is not a security boundary. With the hardcoded RSA key (JS-AUDIT-003) an attacker can mint a B2B token, then POST {orderLinesData: "<payload>"} to /b2b/v2/orders and obtain RCE. Even without escape, infinite-loop / billion-laughs style payloads trigger reliable DoS (the code itself acknowledges this with rceOccupyChallenge).
findings.recommended_fix: Do not evaluate user-supplied code. Accept structured JSON only and validate against a schema. If business logic genuinely requires expressions, run them in a separate sandboxed worker with strict CPU/memory budgets — preferably as data, not code.

findings.title: Zip-Slip / unbounded extraction in /file-upload (.zip handler)
findings.exploitation_rationale: The check `absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.'))` only ensures the resolved path *contains* the project root; it does NOT prevent traversal. An entry path like `../../../../../<projectRoot>/etc/whatever` would still satisfy the check on some layouts. Even if traversal were blocked, the stream destination uses the raw `fileName` from the zip entry (line 45) without sanitisation, so symlink overwrite of files under uploads/complaints is possible. There is also no decompressed-size limit (zip-bomb DoS).

proof_of_concept.step: Craft zip with entry `../../../../../../tmp/pwn` and POST /file-upload.

findings.recommended_fix: Resolve each entry path with path.resolve(extractRoot, fileName) then require startsWith(extractRoot + path.sep). Enforce per-entry and total decompressed-size limits. Reject symlinks and absolute paths.

findings.title: XXE via libxml.parseXml(data, { noent: true })
findings.code_snippet: vm.runInContext('libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true })', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })
findings.exploitation_rationale: `noent: true` enables external entity expansion. The code itself parses the result for /etc/passwd-style content (matchesEtcPasswdFile) confirming exploitability. Used for arbitrary local file disclosure (SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd") and for billion-laughs DoS — both are acknowledged in source (xxeFileDisclosureChallenge / xxeDosChallenge).
findings.recommended_fix: Pass `noent: false`, disable DTD/external entities, or migrate to a JSON ingest. libxmljs2 also exposes a `replaceEntities` flag.

findings.title: Unsafe YAML deserialization (yaml.load) with no size limit
findings.exploitation_rationale: js-yaml.load (vs safeLoad) historically supported custom tags that could instantiate JS objects; even modern versions allow YAML anchors causing exponential reference expansion (yaml-bomb). The route already detects yamlBombChallenge confirming DoS exploitability. The 200KB upload limit (server.ts:681) only bounds linear input — billion-laughs amplification is post-decode.
findings.recommended_fix: Use yaml.load with FAILSAFE_SCHEMA or yaml.safeLoad; reject anchors; cap output size; consider migrating to JSON.

findings.title: Admin role mass-assignment on POST /api/Users
findings.owasp_api_top10: API6:2023-Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
findings.code_snippet:
app.post('/api/Users', verify.registerAdminChallenge())
... // finale auto-CRUD permits any field in req.body to be persisted, including 'role'
existing_mitigations: UserModel role validator restricts to enum
findings.exploitation_rationale: Registration is unauthenticated and goes through finale auto-CRUD. The model permits role in {customer, deluxe, accounting, admin}, and there is no whitelist of registration-permitted attributes. Posting {email, password, role:'admin'} creates an admin account directly. server.ts:407-419 even includes `registerAdminChallenge()` to *detect* this exploitation, confirming the route is exploitable.
findings.recommended_fix: Whitelist fields on registration (email, password only). Hardcode role='customer' server-side. Use finale's `excludeAttributes` for write paths or wrap with a dedicated registration handler.

findings.title: Finale auto-CRUD exposes write-access to every model
findings.code_snippet: for (const { name, exclude, model } of autoModels) { finale.resource({ model, endpoints: [`/api/${name}s`, `/api/${name}s/:id`], excludeAttributes: exclude, pagination: false }) }
findings.reachability: external (rate of auth varies per model)
existing_mitigations: selective denyAll on a subset of routes (Challenges/Hints/Quantitys)
findings.exploitation_rationale: Finale exposes full CRUD on User, Product, Feedback, BasketItem, Complaint, Recycle, SecurityQuestion, SecurityAnswer, Address, PrivacyRequest, Card, Hint. Authorization is bolted on per-endpoint with a fragile mix of denyAll/isAuthorized; any missing entry (e.g. PUT /api/Products/:id is commented out at line 369) immediately becomes a privilege escalation. Customers can PATCH products, set their own prices, etc. The model itself permits role admin (see JS-AUDIT-014).
findings.recommended_fix: Replace finale auto-CRUD with explicit hand-written routes and per-attribute authorization. At minimum, default-deny and explicitly enable only listed methods/fields.

findings.title: Directory listing of /ftp, /encryptionkeys, /support/logs
findings.code_snippet:
app.use('/ftp', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('ftp', { icons: true }))
app.use('/encryptionkeys', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/support/logs', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('logs', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
findings.exploitation_rationale: Three sensitive directories are publicly browseable: ftp/ contains incident-support.kdbx (KeePass db), package.json.bak, coupons_2013.md.bak, suspicious_errors.yml, encrypt.pyc; encryptionkeys/ exposes the JWT public key (used for forgery alongside JS-AUDIT-003) and premium.key; logs/ exposes raw morgan access logs. Combined with the file-serving routes' permissive allowlist (servePublicFiles allows .md/.pdf or the literal name 'incident-support.kdbx'), this is a direct data exfiltration channel.
findings.recommended_fix: Remove serveIndex calls and the public mounts entirely. Move secrets out of the repo. Restrict any operational endpoints to internal networks with auth.

findings.title: SSRF + stored XSS chain in /profile/image/url
findings.exploitation_rationale: The user-supplied `imageUrl` is fetched server-side with no scheme, host, or DNS restriction. An attacker can probe internal services (cloud metadata at 169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, internal admin panels, redis, etc.) and either receive the body back via image content or use timing/error oracles. If fetch fails, the URL is then stored as the user's profileImage and rendered into CSP/HTML — yielding stored XSS via the CSP-string injection chain with JS-AUDIT-009.
findings.recommended_fix: Validate URL: require https scheme, resolve DNS and reject RFC1918/loopback/link-local. Use an outbound proxy with allowlist. Re-encode/store only the resulting image bytes, never the raw URL.

findings.title: NoSQL $where JavaScript injection in trackOrder
existing_mitigations: id is truncated to 60 chars when reflectedXssChallenge is enabled; otherwise stripped to /[^\w-]+/ — but with challenges disabled it falls through to truncation only
findings.exploitation_rationale: MarsDB's $where passes the string into a Function() constructor (Dependabot DEP-005 "Command Injection in marsdb"). With the 60-char truncated path the attacker can send `' || 1 || '` to dump all orders, or supply `'; return process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('id'); //` style payloads for remote code execution inside the Node process. CodeQL alerts 22/23 flag the same.
findings.recommended_fix: Replace MarsDB with a maintained store; do not use $where; query with a strict equality predicate on validated ids.

findings.title: NoSQL $where JavaScript injection in showProductReviews
existing_mitigations: id truncated to 40 chars when challenge enabled, otherwise coerced to Number
findings.exploitation_rationale: Same MarsDB $where issue. An attacker can drive the server into a long-running blocking sleep (the route exposes a global `sleep` helper, deliberately for DoS), exfiltrate timing oracles, and combined with prototype walking, reach process objects.
findings.recommended_fix: Replace MarsDB / drop $where. Validate `id` is an integer.

findings.title: Mass update via multi:true with body-controlled filter (review forgery)
findings.code_snippet: db.reviewsCollection.update({ _id: req.body.id }, { $set: { message: req.body.message } }, { multi: true })
findings.exploitation_rationale: The filter `_id` is whatever the user sends — including operators like `{$ne: null}` which match every document. With multi:true a single PATCH overwrites every review in the database. No ownership check (review.author == user.email) is performed.
findings.recommended_fix: Coerce req.body.id to a string and add `{ author: user.data.email }` to the filter. Drop multi:true.

findings.title: IDOR: body-controlled UserId determines wallet balance reads/writes
findings.code_snippet:
const wallet = await WalletModel.findOne({ where: { UserId: req.body.UserId } })
... WalletModel.increment({ balance: req.body.balance }, { where: { UserId: req.body.UserId } })
existing_mitigations: security.appendUserId() middleware *sets* req.body.UserId — but the client can still send their own value and Express body-parser merges body fields
findings.exploitation_rationale: appendUserId() writes req.body.UserId, but body fields submitted by the client overwrite/merge. By POSTing {UserId: <victimId>, balance: 1000000} the attacker drains/credits arbitrary wallets. The same body field also controls the SELECT and the card ownership check.
findings.recommended_fix: Derive UserId server-side from the JWT *after* clearing any client-supplied value. Reject requests that contain UserId in body.

findings.title: IDOR in /rest/user/data-export via body-supplied UserId
findings.code_snippet: memories = await MemoryModel.findAll({ where: { UserId: req.body.UserId } })
findings.exploitation_rationale: MemoryModel query uses req.body.UserId (mass-assigned by appendUserId() but client-overridable). Attacker harvests other users' memories. Orders/reviews are queried by the logged-in email (better) but the memories leak is sufficient for a privacy breach.
findings.recommended_fix: Use loggedInUser.data.id; ignore body.UserId.

findings.title: IDOR on /api/Addresss/:id via body UserId
findings.code_snippet: AddressModel.findOne({ where: { id: req.params.id, UserId: req.body.UserId } })
findings.exploitation_rationale: Same pattern: appendUserId puts UserId in body but the body can be tampered with. Read/delete arbitrary addresses.
findings.recommended_fix: Pull UserId from the JWT only.

findings.title: IDOR + free-deluxe upgrade in /rest/deluxe-membership
findings.code_snippet: const user = await UserModel.findOne({ where: { id: req.body.UserId, role: security.roles.customer } })
findings.exploitation_rationale: Body-supplied UserId + the paymentMode branch only checks card/wallet if those modes are *named*. Omitting paymentMode skips payment entirely and still promotes the chosen UserId to 'deluxe', as the route's freeDeluxeChallenge helper confirms.
findings.recommended_fix: Authoritative UserId from JWT; require a payment mode; transactionally debit + upgrade.

findings.title: Conditional current-password check + CSRF + password-in-query in changePassword
findings.code_snippet:
if (currentPassword && security.hash(currentPassword) !== loggedInUser.data.password) { ... }
... await user.update({ password: newPasswordInString })
findings.exploitation_rationale: If `current` is absent the check is *skipped entirely*, allowing any session holder to set a new password without proving knowledge of the old one. The route is GET (line uses req.query — confirmed by CodeQL alerts 14-16 'js/sensitive-get-query'), so any cross-origin `` performs the change with the victim's ambient cookie token. No CSRF protection. Passwords also end up in browser history, proxies, and access logs.
findings.recommended_fix: Change method to POST/PATCH, require current password, add CSRF protection (SameSite=strict cookies + CSRF token / double-submit). Read password from body, never the query string.

findings.title: Reset-password rate limiter keys on attacker-controllable X-Forwarded-For
findings.code_snippet: keyGenerator ({ headers, ip }) { return headers['X-Forwarded-For'] ?? ip }
findings.exploitation_rationale: `app.enable('trust proxy')` (line 342) plus a header-derived rate-limit key allows an attacker to rotate X-Forwarded-For on every request, making the limit ineffective. This unblocks security-answer guessing against /rest/user/reset-password (combined with JS-AUDIT-005 hardcoded HMAC secret, full account takeover is feasible).
findings.recommended_fix: Use a trustworthy proxy chain (Express trust proxy with concrete proxy IPs) and key off req.ip post-trust. Add account-level lockout / CAPTCHA.

findings.title: Unauthenticated full configuration dump on /rest/admin/application-configuration
findings.exploitation_rationale: The route is mounted at server.ts:605 with no auth and serializes the entire `config` object — leaking application secrets that may be present in node-config layers (challenge keys, hashes, ctf flags, etc.). 'admin' in the path is purely cosmetic.
findings.recommended_fix: Place behind admin auth; return only non-sensitive subkeys.

findings.title: Sequelize where-injection via JSON.parse(req.params.id) in /api/Recycles/:id
findings.reachability: external_unauthenticated (GET allowed by recycles.getRecycleItem before isAuthorized in server.ts:387)
findings.exploitation_rationale: Parsing `req.params.id` as JSON lets an attacker pass `{"$gt":0}` or other Sequelize operator objects into the where clause, dumping the entire Recycles table. Throws on bad input, but valid JSON objects/operators are accepted directly.
findings.recommended_fix: Coerce id to Number; do not JSON.parse user path parameters.

findings.title: Chatbot remote training data download + dynamic dispatch on botUtils[handler]
findings.exploitation_rationale: If `application.chatBot.trainingData` is configured to a URL, the server fetches *unauthenticated* code-influencing data over the network with no integrity check (no checksum/signature). Anyone able to MITM or compromise the host serving the training data can change bot intents — including ones that influence the `handler` dispatch through botUtils. CodeQL flags the property-injection pattern (alerts 50-54).
findings.recommended_fix: Pin and SHA-256 verify training assets; load from a trusted, immutable URL or repository; whitelist `response.handler` against a constant map.

findings.title: Hard-coded Alchemy WebSocket API key for Ethereum Sepolia
findings.exploitation_rationale: Alchemy API key is committed to the repo. Anyone can quota-drain or abuse the project's Alchemy account. The same key appears in routes/nftMint.ts:16. While Sepolia is testnet, billing/throttling still applies and the owner is responsible.
findings.recommended_fix: Load from env (process.env.ALCHEMY_KEY); rotate the leaked key immediately.

findings.title: Wide-open CORS — `app.use(cors())` with no origin allow-list
findings.exploitation_rationale: Default cors() reflects the request Origin and responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin matching any caller. Combined with cookie-based JWT (security.updateAuthenticatedUsers reads req.cookies.token) and bearer tokens in localStorage, any third-party page can read responses cross-origin. This nullifies same-origin protections for the entire API.
findings.recommended_fix: Configure cors() with an explicit origins allow-list (production frontend host only), credentials:false unless required, and a strict allowed-methods/headers list.

findings.title: Slash-collapse middleware breaks downstream path-based protections
findings.exploitation_rationale: Globally rewriting req.url after Express parses it can desynchronise router matching from the URL the application thinks it is serving (e.g. for swagger, static files, and HTTP cache keys). Combined with the redirect/file-server allowlists that match on substrings, this provides additional bypass surface.
findings.recommended_fix: Use express-slash or perform path normalisation at the edge / reverse proxy, not after routing decisions.

findings.title: Dev `errorhandler` middleware mounted in production — stack trace exposure
findings.exploitation_rationale: The `errorhandler` package is intended for development; it renders full HTML stack traces including local file paths, source lines, and stack frames. It is mounted unconditionally. Many routes intentionally call next(err) on edge cases, leaking implementation detail and aiding further attack. CodeQL js/stack-trace-exposure flags individual instances too (alerts 7-13).
findings.recommended_fix: Mount only when process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development'; use a JSON error responder in production that hides stack frames.

findings.title: Unauthenticated /metrics endpoint reveals internal request topology and labels
findings.exploitation_rationale: Prometheus metrics include process metrics (memory, CPU, event-loop lag), request counts/labels (revealing endpoints), and a custom file-upload histogram. Useful for an attacker mapping the API surface and confirming successful exploitation (e.g. when memory spikes on yaml-bomb).
findings.recommended_fix: Bind /metrics to an internal listener or require admin auth / IP allowlist.

findings.title: Path traversal in /encryptionkeys/:file, /support/logs/:file, /ftp/:file
findings.exploitation_rationale: Disallowing only '/' is insufficient — '%2e%2e%2f' and other encodings, and on some platforms '\\', bypass the filter. servePublicFiles additionally allows the literal `incident-support.kdbx`, exposing a KeePass database, and uses `cutOffPoisonNullByte` to enable %00-truncation attacks on its extension allowlist (so attacker can serve any file by appending `.md%00`). serveLogFiles and serveKeyFiles use the same naive `/`-only guard.
findings.recommended_fix: Use a strict allowlist of filenames or `decodeURIComponent` + `path.normalize` + startsWith(root) check + explicit extension whitelist.

findings.title: Template object injection / LFR via dataErasure layout parameter
findings.exploitation_rationale: req.body is spread into hbs locals and req.body.layout influences template lookup. A targeted `layout` value can pull arbitrary file content into the rendered view (the code itself solves lfrChallenge). The denylist is substring-based and case-toggled, so encodings or unrelated paths still slip through.
findings.recommended_fix: Do not allow client to pick the layout. Use a fixed layout name.

findings.title: Payment card numbers stored as plaintext (INTEGER) without tokenization
findings.code_snippet: cardNum: { type: DataTypes.INTEGER, validate: { isInt: true, min: 1000000000000000, max: 9999999999999998 } }
findings.exploitation_rationale: Full 16-digit PANs stored as integers — no tokenization, no encryption-at-rest, and no PCI segregation. Combined with the UNION SQLi (JS-AUDIT-002) every card becomes exfiltratable. This would be a PCI-DSS violation on day one.
findings.recommended_fix: Do not store PANs. Tokenize via a PCI-DSS-compliant processor (Stripe/Adyen). At minimum, store only last 4 digits + a vault token.

findings.title: Race condition in lib/codingChallenges.ts file caching
findings.code_snippet: if ((await fs.lstat(currPath)).isDirectory()) { ... } else { const code = await fs.readFile(currPath, 'utf8') ... }
findings.exploitation_rationale: CodeQL js/file-system-race identifies a TOCTOU between lstat and readFile. Practically the path is the repo's own source tree and runs at boot from a trusted FS, so impact is low. Worth fixing for correctness.
findings.recommended_fix: Use one fs.stat-and-open call; or simply attempt readFile and catch EISDIR.

findings.title: Logging of cheat scores includes user-controlled challenge metadata (log injection)
findings.exploitation_rationale: CodeQL flagged log-injection in lib/challengeUtils.ts (alert 49). The challenge.key here is internal not user-input, so direct injection is limited. Per the engagement rules we exclude pure log-injection findings — marking needs_review only because logs are publicly served via /support/logs (see JS-AUDIT-016) which gives the otherwise-low finding a real exfil pathway.
findings.recommended_fix: JSON-encode user-influenced log fields; do not expose logs publicly.

findings.title: Hard-coded private mnemonic + secret comparison without constant-time check in checkKeys
findings.code_snippet:
const mnemonic = 'purpose betray marriage blame crunch monitor spin slide donate sport lift clutch'
const mnemonicWallet = HDNodeWallet.fromPhrase(mnemonic)
findings.exploitation_rationale: An HD wallet mnemonic is committed to source. While intentionally used as a challenge target, in production this constitutes an immediate full key compromise. Equality check on the private key uses ===, which is timing-leaking (low risk but worth fixing).
findings.recommended_fix: Do not store HD mnemonics in code. Use crypto.timingSafeEqual for any secret comparison.

findings.title: Multer 1.4.5-lts.1 vulnerable to multiple DoS/CVE advisories
existing_mitigations: 200KB fileSize limit (does not prevent malformed-request crash)
findings.exploitation_rationale: Dependabot lists 6 DoS advisories for multer<2.1.1 including CVE-2026-3520, all of which crash or memory-leak the Node process on crafted multipart requests. /file-upload, /profile/image/file, /rest/memories are all reachable on any deployment. Engagement guidance flags pure DoS as informational normally, but in a real production deployment this is a high-priority availability bug.
findings.recommended_fix: Upgrade to multer >= 2.1.1.

findings.title: sanitize-html 1.4.2 — XSS bypass + ReDoS + IDN bypass
findings.reachability: external (used by lib/insecurity.ts on user-supplied content stored in DB)
findings.exploitation_rationale: Project pins sanitize-html@1.4.2 (Dependabot alerts 2,3,6,7,11,12,18). Multiple XSS-bypass and ReDoS advisories apply. The function is called on usernames/emails in models/user.ts and is also used by sanitizeSecure for legacy XSS protection. This directly enables persistent XSS in the storefront.
findings.recommended_fix: Upgrade to sanitize-html >= 2.12.1 and lock to a strict allow-list of tags/attrs. Consider DOMPurify on the frontend.

findings.title: marsdb — unmaintained, $where -> Function() command injection
findings.exploitation_rationale: Dependabot alert 5 'Command Injection in marsdb' — `DocumentMatcher` selectors on $where pass through `new Function(...)`. With no maintainer fix, the only mitigation is to eliminate $where usage (currently used in trackOrder/showProductReviews/likeProductReviews paths).
findings.recommended_fix: Replace marsdb. Eliminate $where. Migrate review/order data to Sequelize/SQLite.

findings.title: socket.io 3.1.2 — unhandled error event crash
findings.exploitation_rationale: Dependabot alert 13: a crafted Socket.IO packet triggers an unhandled 'error' event killing the Node process. server.ts wires registerWebsocketEvents which uses socket.io.
findings.recommended_fix: Upgrade to socket.io >= 4.6.2, attach socket.on('error') handlers.

findings.title: file-type 16.5.4 vulnerable to ASF infinite-loop DoS
findings.reachability: external_unauthenticated (profile/image/file upload feeds bytes to fileType.fromBuffer)
findings.exploitation_rationale: Dependabot 22: a 55-byte malformed ASF header stalls the event loop indefinitely. profileImageFileUpload pipes the user-supplied buffer through fileType.fromBuffer. Stalling the single Node thread blocks all requests.
findings.recommended_fix: Upgrade to file-type >= 21.3.1 (note ESM change in 17+; may require code adjustments).

findings.title: Polynomial ReDoS in profileImageUrlUpload SSRF detection regex
findings.exploitation_rationale: CodeQL flagged js/polynomial-redos. The repeated `(.)*` is polynomial, not exponential, and the route already incurs a network fetch right after, so any ReDoS spike is dwarfed by the SSRF I/O. Per engagement rules ReDoS without demonstrated impact is excluded. Marking as false_positive while the underlying SSRF (JS-AUDIT-017) carries the real risk.
findings.recommended_fix: Replace with `url.includes('solve/challenges/server-side')`.

findings.title: currentUser allows client-selected fields (password leak via ?fields=password)
findings.code_snippet: for (const field of requestedFields) { if (user?.data[field as keyof typeof user.data] !== undefined) { baseUser[field] = user?.data[field as keyof typeof user.data] } }
findings.exploitation_rationale: The /rest/user/whoami endpoint lets the caller pick which user-object fields are returned via `?fields=...`. There is no allow-list, so `?fields=password,totpSecret` returns the user's MD5 hash and TOTP secret. The route even includes a `passwordHashLeakChallenge` that solves on success, proving exploitability.
findings.recommended_fix: Replace dynamic field projection with a static, allow-listed subset (id, email, profileImage, lastLoginIp).

confirmed_packages.name: notevil
confirmed_packages.note: Unmaintained sandbox; multiple historical escape PoCs.

secrets_in_repo.remediation: Rotate Alchemy key; load from env.
Rotate Alchemy key; load from env.
Remove; use ephemeral wallet for challenge.

secrets_in_repo.type: Public key matching the in-repo RSA private key
Static CTF HMAC key used for flag generation

secrets_in_repo.remediation: Remove from repo and rotate any contained credentials.
